retrieved_docs.title: Viral Hepatitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The majority of cases of viral hepatitis result from hepatotropic viruses A, B, C, D, and E. It is unclear whether the hepatitis G virus (HGV) is pathogenic in humans. Other less common causes of viral hepatitis are cytomegalovirus (CMV), Epstein-Barr virus(EBV), herpes simplex virus (HSV), and varicella-zoster virus (VZV). These are non-heterotropic viruses that usually do not primarily target the liver and rarely cause hepatitis in the immunocompetent state. Hepatitis A, B, C, and D are endemic to the United States, with hepatitis A, B, and C viruses causing 90% of acute viral hepatitis and hepatitis C being the most common cause of chronic hepatitis.

retrieved_docs.title: Viral Hepatitis -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: With human pegivirus-1, most patients are asymptomatic in acute infection with normal aminotransferase levels. Fulminant hepatic failure and chronic infections are rare, and often, the etiology of HPgV-1 is in question. Although coinfection with HBV and HCV is common, it does not increase the severity of the disease. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Viral Hepatitis -- Etiology -- Hepatitis G-Human Pegivirus
retrieved_docs.content: Hepatitis G virus ((HGV), now designated human pegivirus (HPgV-1), is an RNA virus and is a member of the Flaviviridae family. The primary mode of transmission is through infected blood and blood products, but sexual contact and vertical transmission occur as well. It is often manifested as a coinfection in those who have chronic hepatitis B or hepatitis C infections. It has been implicated in patients with acute and chronic liver disease, but the evidence to date has not established it as an agent that causes hepatitis by itself. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Liver Disease -- Etiology -- Chronic Viral Hepatitis
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic hepatitis B, C, and D infections are the most common causes of chronic liver disease in East Asia and Sub-Saharan Africa. There are various genotypes of hepatitis C. In Europe and North America, genotype 1a and 1b are more prevalent, while in Southeast Asia, genotype 3 is more common. A molecular epidemiological study revealed a high prevalence of HCV genotype 4, subtype 4a among Egyptian patients living in Sharkia governorate, Egypt. Chronic hepatitis C, if not treated, may lead to hepatocellular carcinoma.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute on Chronic Liver Failure -- Treatment / Management -- Specific Treatment when Etiology is Known
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with hepatitis A- or E-associated ACLF should receive supportive care, as there are currently no effective antiviral therapies for these infections. Management focuses on monitoring for complications and maintaining organ support. [30] [31] For patients with acute hepatitis C or reactivation of chronic hepatitis C, appropriate antiviral therapy should be initiated. The choice of regimen depends on prior treatment history and the patient’s overall clinical status. Patients experiencing acute hepatitis B or reactivation of chronic hepatitis B should be started on nucleos(t)ide analogs. Early antiviral intervention can help reduce viral load and improve outcomes. In cases where ACLF is triggered by acute decompensation of Wilson disease or hepatic vein thrombosis (such as Budd-Chiari syndrome), liver transplantation should be considered promptly. These conditions carry a poor prognosis with medical therapy alone.

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatitis D -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: In individuals who are chronic carriers of HBsAg, a full-blown superinfection can occur, which may present as severe acute hepatitis or exacerbation of preexisting chronic HBV. In patients with chronic HBV infection, acute HDV infection may be mistaken for an HBV virus flare. In those with undiagnosed HBV infection, clinical presentation and initial investigations may be mistaken for acute HBV infection if HDV superinfection is not entertained as a diagnostic possibility. The clinical course during a superinfection is often more severe than HBV/HDV coinfection. As the presence of HBsAg allows for continuous viral replication, 90% of these individuals progress to chronic HDV. Chronic HDV infection causes more severe morbidity and complications (progressive fibrosis, cirrhosis, hepatocellular carcinoma, and hepatic decompensation) than chronic HBV infection. [12] In cases of triple infection with HBV,  hepatitis C virus (HCV), and HDV, either HDV or HCV will dominate the other viruses depending on the geographic region, host immune factors, activity, and genotype of the HDV involved.

retrieved_docs.title: Viral Hepatitis -- History and Physical -- Human Pegivirus
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with HPgV-1 infection can have a mild infection without jaundice, but most patients are asymptomatic. There have been few reports of fulminant and chronic hepatitis as well as hepatic fibrosis also, but it is not typical and not clearly due to HPgV-1 infection. The patient may also have an elevation of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) and gamma-glutamyl transpeptidase (GGT), but most patients infected with HPgV-1 have normal liver function tests. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Viral Hepatitis -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with chronic HBV infection who later acquire superimposed HDV infection generally develop chronic HDV infection. This typically results in the development of more severe hepatitis compared to patients who only have chronic HBV infection. Many of the coinfected patients will progress to end-stage liver disease and cirrhosis. [56]

Hepatitis is classified as اِلْتِهَابُ الكَبِد, meaning that hepatitis represents a specific type of liver inflammation often caused by viral infection, autoimmune processes, or toxic insults. Together, these relationships highlight that liver inflammation can arise both from alcohol dependence and from hepatitis, underscoring the importance of evaluating alcohol use and identifying hepatitis when assessing patients with hepatic inflammatory conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 655
retrieved_docs.content: Antivirals for treatment of HCV infections include ribavirin and telaprevir, both of which are employed as therapeutic agents against hepatitis C virus. Remdesivir has been identified as a medication that may treat عدوى فيروسية (viral infection), while ribavirin is also noted for its potential to prevent عدوى فيروسية. Together, these antiviral drugs illustrate the spectrum of strategies used to both treat and prevent viral diseases, underscoring their importance in clinical management of infections such as hepatitis C and other viral pathogens.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2620
retrieved_docs.content: Alcoholic hepatitis (تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ) is a liver disorder caused by excessive alcohol consumption that can progress to hepatic cirrhosis (تَشَمُّع كَبِدي). This relationship highlights the potential evolution from an inflammatory, potentially reversible condition to an irreversible stage of extensive liver scarring, emphasizing the importance of early detection and treatment of alcoholic hepatitis to prevent the development of cirrhosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 210

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2274
retrieved_docs.content: Alcoholic cirrhosis (تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ) is a specific subtype of liver cirrhosis (تَشَمُّع كَبِدي). Liver cirrhosis, in turn, is clinically associated with esophageal variceal bleeding (نزيف دَوالِي المريء). Recognizing that patients with alcoholic cirrhosis develop the broader condition of liver cirrhosis helps clinicians anticipate the high risk of variceal hemorrhage, prompting appropriate screening, monitoring, and preventive interventions to improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2676
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of the liver and intrahepatic bile duct manifests as سرطان الخلايا الكبدية (liver cell carcinoma), which is broadly related to مَرَض كَبِدِيّ صَفْراوِيّ (jaundiced liver disease). This connection indicates that hepatocellular carcinoma often presents within the spectrum of liver diseases that cause jaundice, highlighting the importance of recognizing jaundice as a potential clinical clue to underlying malignant liver pathology. Understanding this relationship aids clinicians in early detection, accurate diagnosis, and appropriate management of patients with liver‑related malignancies and associated jaundice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 1151
retrieved_docs.content: Hepatitis (اِلْتِهَابُ الكَبِد) is classified as liver inflammation. It is clinically associated with both alcohol‑abstinence syndrome (متلازمة الامتناع عن تعاطي الكحول) and hepatocellular carcinoma (سرطان الخلايا الكبدية). Hepatocellular carcinoma is a manifestation of cancer of the liver and intrahepatic bile duct and also has a broader relationship with cholestatic liver disease (مَرَض كَبِدِيّ صَفْراوِيّ). These interconnections highlight the role of hepatitis in the development of liver cancer and related hepatic disorders, emphasizing the importance of monitoring and managing hepatitis to prevent progression to malignancy and other liver complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1206
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of liver and intrahepatic bile duct is a malignant condition that manifests as سرطان الخلايا الكبدية (liver cell cancer). Hepatitis (اِلْتِهَابُ الكَبِد), which is classified as liver inflammation, is clinically associated with سرطان الخلايا الكبدية, indicating that patients with hepatitis may have an increased risk of developing this type of liver cancer. Moreover, سرطان الخلايا الكبدية has a broader relationship with مَرَض كَبِدِيّ صَفْراوِيّ, a jaundiced liver disease, suggesting that liver cell cancer can be part of or contribute to the clinical spectrum of jaundice‑related hepatic disorders. Recognizing the interconnections among cancer of liver and intrahepatic bile duct, hepatitis, and مَرَض كَبِدِيّ صَفْراوِيّ is essential for comprehensive diagnosis and management of hepatic pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 285
retrieved_docs.content: Ribavirin is classified as a member of the group of antivirals used for the treatment of hepatitis C virus (HCV) infections, indicating its role within this therapeutic class. In addition to its use against HCV, ribavirin may also help prevent viral infections (عدوى فيروسية). Remdesivir, another antiviral agent, may be employed to treat viral infections (عدوى فيروسية). Together, ribavirin and remdesivir exemplify antiviral medications that are utilized both for treating specific viral diseases such as HCV and for broader management of viral infections, underscoring their importance in antiviral therapy and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 149
retrieved_docs.content: Antivirals for treatment of HCV infections are classified as direct acting antivirals, a category that also includes cyclic amines, direct acting antivirals. Within the HCV‑specific antiviral class, telaprevir is identified as a member, representing a targeted therapy for hepatitis C virus infection. Cyclic amines, direct acting antivirals comprise compounds such as rimantadine, which is recognized as a member of this subclass. Rimantadine exerts a physiologic effect characterized by alteration of cellular synthetic activity, reflecting its impact on host cell metabolism. Together, these relationships illustrate how specific antiviral agents are organized within broader mechanistic classes and how individual drugs can produce distinct cellular effects relevant to their therapeutic use.
